nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater ponds, lakes, ditches, and slow-moving streams; floats on the surface of nutrient-rich still or sluggish water in temperate and tropical climates (absent only from arctic/subarctic zones).

avoidWith: tanks with submerged plants requiring high light

summary: One of the world's smallest flowering plants. Tiny floating fronds (1–8 mm) carpet the water surface, stripping nitrate/phosphate and shading fry and shrimp. Hardy and effortless to grow — but INVASIVE: virtually impossible to fully eradicate once introduced.
careNotes: Lemna minor (Common Duckweed) is among the smallest flowering plants on Earth, consisting of 1–8 mm oval fronds with a single 1–2 cm root dangling underwater. It floats freely on the surface and reproduces almost exclusively by vegetative budding — colonies can double in 2–3 days under good conditions, rapidly forming a dense green carpet. Lighting can be low to high; it tolerates virtually any aquarium illumination. CO2 is not required (the plant takes carbon from air). It is a powerful nutrient stripper, removing ammonia, nitrate, and phosphate, making it excellent for shrimp tanks, fry-rearing setups, and as a natural filter aid. Surface agitation must be low — strong filter flow or aggressive surface skimmers will push fronds against the glass, where they rot. CAUTION: duckweed is widely considered invasive in aquariums. Even a single frond will re-establish a colony, and it spreads via splashes, nets, plants, and even on fingertips. Once introduced it is virtually impossible to fully remove. Equally important, a dense surface mat BLOCKS LIGHT to plants below — incompatible with high-light planted tanks containing carpeting species or stem plants. Manual skimming (a fine net or a yogurt-cup scoop) is the standard control method; goldfish and koi will eat it readily. Tolerates 6–33°C in the wild; aquarium range 15–30°C is comfortable.
breedingNotes: Reproduces almost entirely by vegetative budding — each frond produces daughter fronds from lateral pouches, doubling the colony every 2–3 days under good light and nutrients. Flowering is extremely rare in cultivation and not relevant to propagation. To start a new tank, transfer even a single frond; growth will fill the surface within weeks.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Lemna minor
primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales), native range, frond size (1–8 mm), water parameters (pH 6.5–8; temperature 6–33°C in wild), propagation, and invasiveness via bird dispersal.
